rationale: In the introduction, Frederick, Lee, and Baskin (2014) is used as a direct contradictory prior result, noting that it questioned whether attraction effects exist under rich/sensory option descriptions. That contrast helps motivate the citing paper's core objective of identifying when context effects do or do not emerge. The citing paper then positions its findings within the same robustness and boundary-conditions debate, so the citation plays a meaningful framing and positioning role rather than a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper treats the cited paper as important counterevidence on attraction-effect robustness and uses it to motivate a more conditional account of context effects. It refines this debate by proposing and testing a different moderator structure (advantaged vs. disadvantaged targets and decision strategy hierarchy) for when attraction/extremeness effects appear.
